Do global awards actually drive Japanese wine sales?

Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
Approximately 70% of Japan's 400+ wineries are family-owned, often too busy with daily operations to pursue exports or global awards.
In 2024, Suntory From Farm Tomi Koshu 2022 won 'Best in Show' at Decanter World Wide Awards, the highest accolade.
Repeat international award winners are typically larger producers like Château Mercian, Grace Wine, and Katsunuma Jyozo, not small family wineries.
Katsunuma Jyozo's 2003-2004 Vinalies Internationales medals led to an export deal with Bernard Magrez Group, expanding into European markets.
Château Mercian executive credits restaurant placements and high-profile events like the 2016 G7 Summit—not awards alone—as key to overseas success.

https://vinepair.com/articles/champagne-flavor-map-infographic/
The Ultimate Champagne Flavor Map [Infographic]

Wineries of all types strive to develop an estate identity through their liquid, but nowhere is that brand recognition as important as it is in Champagne. The flagship product for most Champagne maisons is the “house style,” a proprietary blend of grapes from assorted plots and vintages. No two non-vintage recipes are the same.
Champagne must be produced in the Champagne region to bear that label; house style is a proprietary blend distinguishing each maison.
Three grapes permitted in Champagne: Chardonnay (mineral, light, elegant), Pinot Noir (richer, fruit-forward), and Pinot Meunier.
Champagne undergoes two fermentations: first in vats, second in bottles to produce bubbles; maturation occurs in bottle, not barrel.
Minimum 12-month lees aging is required; longer aging with dead yeast cells develops brioche and almond flavors, creating creamy texture and fuller body.

Alessia Antinori has been appointed president of Primum Familiae Vini, the association of 12 historic family-owned wine estates, placing culture, architecture and heritage at the centre of its agenda for the year ahead
Alessia Antinori appointed president of Primum Familiae Vini for 2026-2027, representing the 26th generation of Marchesi Antinori.
PFV is an association of 12 historic family-owned wine estates founded in 1991, focused on family ownership and intergenerational succession.
Antinori's presidency theme is Art, Architecture and Wine, emphasizing estates as custodians of cultural and architectural heritage.
Marchesi Antinori traces winemaking history to 1385; Antinori nel Chianti Classico (opened 2012) cited as celebrated contemporary architectural project.
Château Haut-Brion opening new winery designed by Annabelle Selldorf in early 2027.

After The Last Pour
For years, Shanghai's natural wine bars embodied a new generation of drinkers. One of the movement's pioneers, William Zhu, reflects on what was lost, what survived, and why he still believes in wine
SOiF, a Shanghai natural wine bar co-founded by William Zhu, closed its doors after helping drive the city's natural wine movement and bistro boom.
Shanghai's two-month Covid lockdown in 2022 devastated hospitality and collapsed consumer confidence, from which the bar scene never fully recovered.
Uncontrolled expansion of bistros after 2022 diluted quality; many venues adopted the bistro concept for fashion rather than investing in products or building loyal customers.
By late 2023, Shanghai had over 83,000 Dianping results for 'natural wine' versus 26,000 for craft beer, indicating the category's prominence despite market contraction.

https://vino-joy.com/2026/07/10/frances-heatwave-cuts-wine-harvest-as-growers-brace-for-smaller-vintage/
France’s Heatwave Cuts Wine Harvest as Growers Brace for Smaller Vintage
Weeks of drought are reducing yields and raising quality concerns, though weak global demand means shortages are far from certain
France's heatwave is reducing wine harvests across Champagne, Bordeaux, and Burgundy, with Champagne expecting ~10% production decline and Bordeaux potentially down 20% or more.
Extreme heat accelerates grape ripening, risking higher alcohol levels, lower acidity, and imbalanced wines despite appearing ripe.
Champagne may harvest around August 15, potentially the earliest on record; Burgundy around August 20; Bordeaux Crémant as early as August 1.
Smaller global supply unlikely to create widespread shortages due to weak demand and excess inventory, though premium appellations and white wines face tighter supplies.
Burgundy's Premier Cru and Grand Cru whites remain undersupplied, making production declines more impactful there than in village-tier reds.

French heatwave to cut Champagne yields by 10%

France's prolonged summer heat and drought are shrinking grape development across major wine regions, with Champagne growers expecting yields to fall around 10% and harvests beginning weeks earlier than normal
Champagne yields forecast to drop 10% due to prolonged French heatwave and drought.
Champagne harvest expected to begin around August 15, roughly a month earlier than typical decades ago.
Burgundy and Bordeaux producers expect significant output declines but say it's too early to quantify losses precisely.
Prolonged heat increasing sugar accumulation in grapes, potentially affecting alcohol levels and flavor profiles.
Champagne houses may mitigate production impact by drawing on reserve wines despite lower grape yields.

https://daily.sevenfifty.com/mourvedre-was-made-for-heat-rising-temperatures-are-testing-it/
Mourvèdre Was Made for Heat. Rising Temperatures Are Testing It
In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
Mourvèdre, heat-tolerant but late-ripening, is struggling to maintain balance and elegance even in its traditional hot regions as temperatures rise.
Bandol producers are experimenting with canopy management techniques—gobelet training, higher pruning, shade planting—to protect grapes from extreme heat and sunburn.
Irrigation, traditionally avoided in dry-farmed Bandol, is becoming necessary as drought intensifies; some vines died during the 2022-2024 drought in Jumilla.
Jumilla's vineyard surface area halved over 20 years, with only higher-altitude sites (600-800 meters) remaining viable as lower areas were abandoned.
Bandol's cooler sites traditionally reserved for rosé (75% of production) are now being considered for red wine as climate shifts.

Seven organic Sauvignon Blanc for summer sipping
From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
Sauvignon Blanc is tricky to grow organically because it's sensitive to powdery and downy mildew, requiring regular fungicide treatments.
Copper-based Bordeaux mixture is the only permitted organic fungicide for powdery mildew, but the vine's flower is sensitive to copper.
Domaine les Eminades in Languedoc credits nearly 20 years of organic farming and the region's clement climate for their Sauvignon's disease resilience.
Andreas Tscheppe's Blue Dragonfly from south Styria, Austria is fermented with natural yeasts, minimal sulphur, and aged almost two years in large old barrels.

https://themorningclaret.com/p/mountains-and-magliocco-casa-comerci
Mountains and Magliocco
Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
Simon J. Woolf
Calabria has nine DOCs; only Cirò has achieved fame, though the region has grown vines since before Roman times.
Magliocco comes in two variants: Magliocco Dolce (tannic, eastern Calabria) and Magliocco Canino (acidic, late-ripening, potentially Sangiovese-related).
Casa Comerci, a certified organic 27-hectare estate near Nicotera, grows only Magliocco Canino and Greco Bianco, fermenting and aging exclusively in stainless steel tanks.
Magliocco Canino is extremely late-ripening; Casa Comerci's 2017 rosé was harvested in early December, with October harvests normal even today.
Cirò wines dominate local wine lists despite being 200km northeast of the Tyrrhenian coast; winery Librandi has significant market control.

The Sysco-ification of Wine
How wine culture's lazy "drink what you like" ethos has failed, and why it's time to break out the pitchforks
Jason Wilson
Gallo controlled 33% of U.S. wine market in 2023 and has since acquired Rombauer, Hahn Family Wines, Massican, Whiny Baby, and Bev canned wine.
Top 11 wine suppliers controlled 71% of U.S. wine in 2024, according to American Association of Wine Economists data.
Independent wine distributors fell from roughly 3,000 in the 1990s to just over 1,000 today.
Three dominant distributors—Breakthru, RNDC, and Southern Glazer's—controlled 53% of wine and spirits distribution in America as of 2024.
